In Chapter 10, he says punishing soldiers before they are attached makes them disobedient; if not obedient, they are useless. Governance should combine humanity with strict military law. Constant inconsistency in reward and punishment is one of the seven conditions leading to defeat.

questions.query: What analogy does Sun Tzu use to describe the management of soldiers in large numbers vs. small numbers?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu says in Chapter 6 (Energy): 'The control of a large force is the same principle as the control of a few men: it is merely a question of dividing up their numbers.' He also says maneuvering with an army resembles maneuvering with a small force -- it is merely a question of signs and signals (flags and drums). Just as a single body, a great host must be capable of responding to orders as one.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about the five constant factors in relation to a general's knowledge?
questions.ground_truth_answer: In Chapter 1, Sun Tzu says these five factors should be familiar to every general: 'He who knows them will be victorious; he who knows them not will fail.' He uses them as a basis of comparison when seeking to determine military conditions -- comparing which sovereign has the Moral Law, which general has ability, which side has Heaven and Earth advantages, which side has better discipline, stronger armies, better-trained officers, and more consistent rewards and punishments.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say in Chapter 8 (Maneuvering) about the relationship between a general and his troops when maneuvering?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu warns that if a general sends his whole army to contend for advantage, the first arrivals will be fresh but the rearguard exhausted. If he sends part, his men are too few to conquer. He who is foremost to climb the walls is rewarded -- but early assault on walls often loses many men. He counsels understanding the topography, using local guides, and balancing the advantage of rapid movement against the risk of exhausting troops.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about attacking with fire in terms of necessary conditions?
questions.ground_truth_answer: In Chapter 13 (The Attack by Fire), Sun Tzu states that fire attacks require suitable material (inflammable material must be present), the right season (dry weather, low humidity), and appropriate days (when the wind is rising). He gives the four times when fire attacks are most effective and says one must move when advantageous -- if fire is in the enemy's camp, coordinate from outside promptly; if the fire does not achieve its purpose, wait.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about the military virtue of speed (rapidity) in Chapter 12 and elsewhere?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu emphasizes speed throughout The Art of War. In Chapter 12 (The Nine Situations): 'Rapidity is the essence of war; take advantage of the enemy's unreadiness, make your way by unexpected routes, and attack unguarded spots.' In Chapter 2, he argues against prolonged warfare. In Chapter 8 (Maneuvering), he describes marching day and night to surprise the enemy. Speed reduces costs, keeps the initiative, and prevents the enemy from preparing.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about intelligence gathering through the observation of the enemy's behavior in Chapter 10?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu gives extensive behavioral intelligence indicators in Chapter 10: if the enemy offers easy bait, there is an ambush; if his troops move confidently without deliberation, he is under orders from above; if his men look to the right and left, they are hungry; if those who are sent to draw water drink before bringing it back, the army is thirsty; if there is tumult in camp at night, the general is not respected.

questions.query: What is Sun Tzu's philosophy on the costs of war as stated in Chapter 2?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu is explicit about the costs: with 1,000 chariots, 1,000 heavy chariots, and 100,000 mail-clad soldiers, expenditures run a thousand ounces of silver per day. When campaigns are protracted, state resources cannot sustain the strain. He says 'there is no instance of a country having benefited from prolonged warfare.' The skilled soldier does not raise a second levy or load supply wagons more than twice -- victory should be swift.

questions.query: What psychological techniques does Sun Tzu advocate against an enemy commander in Chapter 6 and 7?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu advocates: irritating a choleric enemy to provoke rash action; pretending weakness to encourage his arrogance; depriving him of rest by constant movement; separating him from his allies; striking where he is unprepared; appearing where unexpected. He recommends keeping the enemy ignorant of your plans while discovering his, maintaining initiative by attacking in many places while concentrating against his key weakness.

questions.query: What are the characteristics of 'good fighters' as Sun Tzu describes them in Chapter 5 (Tactical Dispositions)?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu says good fighters of old first put themselves beyond possibility of defeat, then waited for an opportunity to defeat the enemy. Invincibility depends on oneself; the vulnerability of the enemy depends on him. Good fighters can secure themselves against defeat, but cannot make the enemy vulnerable -- that depends on the enemy's mistakes. What the ancient call clever fighting is victory gained before the enemy recognizes the battle is lost.

questions.query: How does Sun Tzu describe the ideal relationship between a general and his sovereign?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu identifies the general's independence from sovereign interference as one of the five essentials for victory: 'He will win who has military capacity and is not interfered with by the sovereign.' In Chapter 9 (Variation in Tactics), he says there are commands of the sovereign not to be obeyed if they conflict with military necessity. Yet Sun Tzu also says the general is the arbiter of the state's fate and acts in the sovereign's interest.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about the use of numerical superiority and maneuver in Chapter 7?
questions.ground_truth_answer: In Chapter 7 (Weak Points and Strong), Sun Tzu says that an army of many cannot make up for lack of skill; if you know how to concentrate and disperse at the right moment, you will always win even if outnumbered. He who knows neither to concentrate nor disperse will be beaten. Concentrate when the enemy is dispersed; strike his center when he is spread out.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu describe as the proper time to use fire attacks in relation to weather conditions?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Tzu specifies in Chapter 13 that fire attacks must be launched in favorable conditions: during dry weather and on days when the wind is rising (not during calm weather). If the wind rises in the day, do not attack at night; if it rises at night, do not attack in the day. Wait for a persistent wind. He names specific constellations (the four times when the moon passes through certain stellar positions) as the best times.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu say about the importance of the 'Middle Kingdom' position or interior lines in Chapter 12?
questions.ground_truth_answer: In Chapter 12 (The Nine Situations) Sun Tzu describes the advantages of whoever holds key intersecting highways or positions -- whoever holds 'ground of intersecting highways' (key to three adjoining states) can dominate all. He who gets there first and fortifies can use it as a pivot. Whoever controls the central position with roads radiating outward can concentrate faster than enemies coming from the periphery.

questions.query: What is the historical context of Sun Tzu according to the biographical information in the introduction?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Sun Wu (Sun Tzu) was a native of the Ch'i State in ancient China. He lived during the Spring and Autumn period (approximately 6th century BC), during the reign of Ho Lu, King of Wu (514-496 BC). He served as a general for Wu, defeating the Ch'u State and entering its capital Ying. He was recommended to Ho Lu by Wu Tzu-hsu and rose to prominence through his demonstration of military discipline. His ancestor was a minister of state in Ch'i.

questions.query: What does the preface of the Art of War say about Ts'ao Ts'ao's contribution to the text?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Ts'ao Ts'ao (Wei Wu Ti, AD 155-220), the famous warlord, wrote a preface and commentary to Sun Tzu. In his preface he praises the work as among the greatest writings on warfare he has read, and notes that his contemporaries failed to grasp the full meaning of Sun Tzu's instructions. His commentary (still preserved) is characterized as terse and military in tone. He 'strung together' Sun Tzu's Art of War, meaning he made an explanatory paraphrase.

questions.query: What does Sun Tzu mean by the phrase 'victorious warriors win first and then go to war, while defeated warriors go to war first and then seek to win'?
questions.ground_truth_answer: This saying (from Chapter 5, Tactical Dispositions) means the superior general decides the outcome before the battle begins -- through superior preparation, intelligence, and positioning -- and then fights in a situation already won. The inferior general fights first and relies on chance for victory. The emphasis is on preparation, knowing the enemy, and choosing the moment to fight from a position of guaranteed advantage.
